coredata.dc:title: Algal carbon quantum dots/Bi2MoO6 S-scheme heterojunction with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic degradation for ciprofloxacin

coredata.dc:description: The formation of S-scheme heterojunctions offers a viable strategy for enhancing photocatalytic efficiency in tackling organic contaminants. However, the green, low-cost, easy-to-synthesize S-scheme heterojunction is critical to further large-scale production. In this study, we successfully developed a new, eco-friendly, and cost-effective S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst, Carbon quantum dots prepared from Sargassum horneri / Bi2MoO6 (SCQDs/BMO) utilizing an in-situ hydrothermal method. Carbon quantum dots derived from marine macroalga Sargassum horneri (SCQDs) are integrated with Bi2MoO6 semiconductors to formulate the groundbreaking catalytic material. The results affirm that SCQDs’ incorporation generated an internal electric field (IEF) at SCQDs-BMO interfaces, significantly enhancing charge separation. Moreover, SCQDs act as electron capturers to enhance carrier separation. At the same time, during the composite process, the carbon dots can reduce Bi3+ to Bi0. The high conductivity of Bi further enhances the electron transport capability of the system, working synergistically with the internal electric field to promote the separation and migration of charge carriers. This optimization improves the interfacial charge transfer pathway between BMO and SCQDs. Thus, SCQDs/BMO exhibits superior photocatalytic degradation for ciprofloxacin (CIP) under visible light, up to 97.7 % in 180 min, whose rate constant is 2.6 times that of BMO. The primary active agents, including ·O2 −/·OH, produced on the surface of SCQDs/BMO are crucial in the photodegradation of CIP. The potential intermediates, degradation pathways, and underlying mechanisms have been clearly outlined. This study offers an efficient and environmentally friendly method of developing high-activity S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts for treating antibiotics wastewater.

head.ce:title: Algal carbon quantum dots/Bi2MoO6 S-scheme heterojunction with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic degradation for ciprofloxacin

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The formation of S-scheme heterojunctions offers a viable strategy for enhancing photocatalytic efficiency in tackling organic contaminants. However, the green, low-cost, easy-to-synthesize S-scheme heterojunction is critical to further large-scale production. In this study, we successfully developed a new, eco-friendly, and cost-effective S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst, Carbon quantum dots prepared from Sargassum horneri / Bi2MoO6 (SCQDs/BMO) utilizing an in-situ hydrothermal method. Carbon quantum dots derived from marine macroalga Sargassum horneri (SCQDs) are integrated with Bi2MoO6 semiconductors to formulate the groundbreaking catalytic material. The results affirm that SCQDs’ incorporation generated an internal electric field (IEF) at SCQDs-BMO interfaces, significantly enhancing charge separation. Moreover, SCQDs act as electron capturers to enhance carrier separation. At the same time, during the composite process, the carbon dots can reduce Bi3+ to Bi0. The high conductivity of Bi further enhances the electron transport capability of the system, working synergistically with the internal electric field to promote the separation and migration of charge carriers. This optimization improves the interfacial charge transfer pathway between BMO and SCQDs. Thus, SCQDs/BMO exhibits superior photocatalytic degradation for ciprofloxacin (CIP) under visible light, up to 97.7 % in 180 min, whose rate constant is 2.6 times that of BMO. The primary active agents, including ·O2 −/·OH, produced on the surface of SCQDs/BMO are crucial in the photodegradation of CIP. The potential intermediates, degradation pathways, and underlying mechanisms have been clearly outlined. This study offers an efficient and environmentally friendly method of developing high-activity S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts for treating antibiotics wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ciprofloxacin, commonly called CIP, is a quintessential example of the quinolone family of antibiotics and ranks among the most widely utilized antibiotics globally [1–3]. Its robust structural integrity and tenacious resilience present significant hurdles for thorough metabolic processing in human and animal systems and for its breakdown in the natural ecosystem. Thus, an efficient method to eliminate CIP from aquatic systems is imperative to mitigate its risks to ecosystems and human well-being. Various technologies for antibiotic wastewater treatment have been developed, such as adsorption [4], membrane filtration [5], electrochemistry [6], and photocatalysis [7], in which photocatalytic processes are extensively employed for purging antibiotics from aquatic sources, attributed to their user-friendliness, efficacy, and affordability [8–10].

Bismuth-containing semiconductors have garnered significant interest due to their unique optical characteristics and the Aurivillius crystal structure [11]. Bi2MoO6 (BMO) stands out among other photocatalysts for its non-toxic nature, straightforward synthesis process, and affordable starting materials, making it a prime candidate for widespread environmental applications. Its distinctive Aurivillius structure, characterized by alternating [Bi2O2]2+ layers and perovskite-style [MoO4]2- layers, has garnered significant attention in photocatalysis. This is primarily due to its inherent asymmetric polarisation center and anisotropic characteristics [12,13]. Moreover, BMO exhibits an adjustable bandgap between 2.5 and 2.8 eV, with extensive light adsorption capabilities [14]. Nevertheless, BMO's photocatalytic performance is suboptimal, attributed to its limited light absorption, inefficient charge carrier separation, and reduced capability for reduction reactions [15–18]. To overcome the previously mentioned shortcomings of BMO photocatalysts, researchers have devised a range of strategies to boost their photocatalytic efficiency. These approaches include creating heterojunctions [19], introducing defects [20], manipulating morphology [16], optimizing structural assessment [21], and incorporation of co-catalysts such as noble metals [22–25].

The S-scheme heterojunction has proven to be an exceptionally effective method for improving the movement and separation of light-activated charge carriers, thereby markedly elevating the photocatalytic efficiency [26]. The S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst comprises an oxidized photocatalytic component (OP) and a reduced photocatalytic component (RP). Electrons from the RP, which possesses a higher Fermi level (Ef), migrate to the OP, which has a lower Ef, until equilibrium is reached. This movement forms an intrinsic electric field (IEF) and induces band curvature at the interface of the two materials [27–29]. Efficiently isolating photogenerated charges is possible when exposed to light, thanks to the interplay of isoelectric focusing and band bending. However, intricate synthesis methods plague the prevalent S-scheme heterojunctions frequently cited in the literature and the requirement for costly or harmful substances. Consequently, there is a pressing demand for creating eco-friendly, affordable, and straightforward-to-produce Bi2MoO6-based S-scheme heterojunctions.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are zero-dimensional, fluorescent, and environmentally friendly catalysts that do not contain metals. Their unique optoelectronic characteristics, combined with low toxicity, affordability, superior light absorption, and biocompatibility, have led to their widespread integration into conventional semiconductor materials to boost photocatalytic efficiency [30–33]. Biomass is ideal for synthesizing CQDs, offering eco-friendliness, copious availability, hetero-atom doping potential, and renewable resources [34]. Recent studies have indicated that carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from biomass can markedly elevate the efficacy of bismuth-based photocatalysts, enhancing their reactivity to visible light and optimizing the separation of charge carriers. Take, for example, a hybrid material made from peach leaf CQDs and Bi2WO6, which showcased exceptional prowess in breaking down tetracycline. Additionally, nitrogen-doped CQDs sourced from reed straw have enhanced the photodegradation process of sulfamethoxazole when integrated into BiO2-x/BiOCl structures [34]. Consequently, biomass-originated CQDs enhance the photocatalytic performance of bismuth-containing photocatalysts, optimizing their efficacy in treating antibiotic-laden wastewater.

In this study, an innovative S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst (SCQDs/BMO) was meticulously crafted and adorned with quantum dots sourced from the marine macroalga Sargassum horneri (S. horneri) and BMO. This novel construct was designed to significantly ramp up the degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) when exposed to visible light, as depicted in Fig. 1 . The SCQDs/BMO exhibited exceptional photocatalytic prowess, which is creditable for forming a modified S-scheme system that incorporates quantum dots, generating an internal electric field that promotes charge separation. A thorough investigation into the elevated photocatalytic process of this heterojunction was carried out, focusing on the dynamics of interfacial photo-carrier transport and reallocation. Moreover, the specific breakdown routes of CIP through the SCQDs/BMO system and an evaluation of the resulting by-products' toxicity were delineated. This research paves the way for crafting highly efficient photocatalysts to purify antibiotic-laden wastewater.

The sample of S. horneri was procured from the coastal waters of Chang dao in Yantai, China. It underwent a meticulous cleaning process using deionized water to remove any substantial contaminants before being subjected to a drying treatment at 80 °C for a full day. Subsequently, it was reduced to a finely milled powder and filtered through a mesh with 100 holes per inch to obtain the appropriate texture. All the chemical agents employed in the research, such as bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3⋅5H2O, ≥ 99.0 %), ethylene glycol (EG, ≥ 99.5 %), sodium molybdate dihydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O, ≥ 99.0 %), and ciprofloxacin hydrochloride (C17H12FO3·HCl·H2O, 98 %), were purchased from Guoyao Co. Ltd. The rest of the chemicals used were of analytical quality and remained unpurified for the experiment. Throughout the procedures, the lab-generated deionized water was the solvent of choice.

S. horneri powder was placed into a beaker with deionized water and ultrasonically disrupted in an ice bath for 180 min. The resulting homogenate was then transferred to a reaction kettle for pyrolysis at 220 °C for 18 h. The centrifuge spun the pyrolysis solution at a brisk 10,000 r/ min for a quarter of 1 h, after which the top layer, or supernatant, was passed through a fine 0.22 μm syringe filter. Post-filtering, the resultant fluid was transferred into a dialysis sack with a 1000 Da cutoff and subjected to dialysis for a full day, with the deionized water being swapped out every 8h to maintain purity. After dialysis, the liquid was frozen for 12 h and then freeze-dried for 48 h to obtain S. horneri carbon quantum dots, denoted as SCQDs.

Na2MoO4·2H2O and Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (w: w = 1:4) were each dissolved separately in beakers with 20 mL of ethylene glycol and stirred until fully dissolved. A solution of Na2MoO4·2H2O was incrementally introduced to the Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, incorporating different quantities of SCQDs (2.0, 3.8, and 5.0 mg) and 30 mL of ethanol anhydrous. The concoction was thoroughly agitated for a half-hour before being subjected to a hydrothermal process at a scalding 160 degrees Celsius for an entire day. Post-reaction, the resultant solution was strained through a fine 0.45-μm filter and then cleansed three to four times with a combination of water and anhydrous ethanol to remove any unwanted particles before being left to air in a 60 ℃ oven. This yielded a series of SCQDs/Bi2MoO6 amalgamations, each with varying concentrations of SCQDs, designated as 0.5CB, 0.75CB, and 1.0CB, respectively.
The characterization techniques of the as-prepared photocatalysts are given in S1 (Text S1).

CIP degradation under visible light exposure was investigated to gauge the efficacy of SCQDs/Bi2MoO6. To simulate sunlight, this research used a 300 W xenon lamp as the artificial lighting source. During a standard procedure, 50 mg of the composite photocatalyst was evenly distributed within 100 mL of a 20 mg/L CIP solution at ambient temperature. The blend was agitated without light for half an hour to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Following this equilibration period, the xenon lamp was activated to shine upon the solution. Aliquots of 1 mL were extracted at predetermined intervals (5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 90, 120, and 180 min) and passed through a 0.22 μm filter before being assessed for their absorbance at 277 nm via a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2600, made in Japan). Moreover, a series of batch tests were carried out to examine the influence of different photocatalyst dosages (ranging from 10 to 50 mg), solution pH levels (spanning from 3 to 11), and initial concentrations of CIP (from 10 to 30 mg/L) on the photocatalytic degradation rates. The degradation efficiency (D%) was determined using Eq (1), and the process followed a pseudo-first-order kinetic model described by Eq. (2).
(1)

C0 denotes the starting concentration of CIP in the solution, while C represents the concentration at time t, with k being the rate constant for the photocatalytic process.

A series of tests were conducted to assess the photocatalyst's resistance to degradation through recycling. At the end of each 180 min photocatalytic degradation cycle, the composite photocatalyst was extracted from the solution, rinsed with anhydrous ethanol and deionized water until a neutral pH was achieved, then dried and utilized again in the following experiments. This process was repeated for a total of three cycles of photocatalytic degradation.

To delve into the function of free radicals within the photocatalytic breakdown of CIP, we introduced radical scavengers, or what we refer to as quenchers, into our photocatalytic setup. In our experimental setup, we employed EDTA-2Na, p-benzoquinone (BQ), isopropanol (IPA), and AgNO3 as our scavengers, each one tailored to neutralize a particular reactive species: holes (h+), superoxide radicals (•O2 −), hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and electrons (e−), respectively. Before commencing the light exposure phase, we introduced 2 mL of each scavenger at a concentration of 100 mmol/L into the degradation solution to serve as traps for these reactive entities.

To thoroughly investigate the by-products yielded by the photocatalytic breakdown process, we delved into the degradation products of the CIP when using a sophisticated composite photocatalyst. We examined this using electrospray ionization (ESI) in positive ion mode on a state-of-the-art liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry system (LC-MS, model 1290II-6460, produced by Agilent in the States). The mobile phase was a blend of two separate solvents, designated as A and B. Solvent A was a combination of 0.1 m acetate and acetic acid, pH-balanced to 5.3, whereas solvent B was pure acetonitrile, and we maintained a flow rate of 0.2 mL/min with the column temperature kept steady at 40 degrees Celsius. For the ESI parameters, the nebulizing gas flow was fixed at 10 L/min with a pressure of 45 psi, and the mass spectrometer was tuned to monitor fragments within the range of 10 to 600 m/z. Risk assessments for the resulting substances were performed using the T.E.S.T. software package.

Fig. 2 Depiction of the intricate microstructure and compositional elements of the newly synthesized specimens. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image in Fig. 2(a) reveals the SCQDs' uniform particle size distribution. By using Nano Measurer to select 100 SCQDs particles from this image randomly, we determined that the average size of the SCQDs clusters around the 2 to 4.5 nm range, as shown in Fig. 2(c), thus affirming the efficacy of the carbon quantum dot synthesis process. Furthermore, the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) snapshots depicting the SCQDs in Fig. 2(b) showcase an interplanar distance of precisely 0.21 nm, which corresponds exactly to the typical spacing found in the graphite (1 0 0) lattice plane [35]. The SEM images of BMO, 0.5CB, 0.75CB, and 1.0CB are depicted in Fig. 2(d-g). The samples 0.5CB, 0.75CB, and 1.0CB all exhibit a three-dimensional spherical configuration characterized by nanorods roughly 1 μm in length. BMO exhibits a three-dimensional spherical structure composed of nanosheets and nanorods, with relatively large particles and a rough surface. After the introduction of SCQDs, the crystal structure of BMO transforms, gradually shifting from nanosheets to nanorods. In the 0.5CB sample, with the incorporation of SCQDs, the composite material shows a transition from nanosheets to nanorods, resulting in a more ordered nanorod morphology compared to pure BMO. As the SCQD content increases, the morphology of the 0.75CB sample becomes finer and more uniform, with the nanorod structure becoming more pronounced. This uniform nanorod structure not only facilitates improved electron mobility but also provides more active sites for subsequent photocatalytic reactions, thereby enhancing its adsorption capacity in dark reactions and boosting photocatalytic performance. In contrast, in the 1.0CB sample, due to the excessive amount of carbon dots, the composite material tends to undergo over-reduction, leading to significant aggregation. This aggregation negatively impacts both its photocatalytic and adsorption properties. The full Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) spectrum is shown in Fig. S1, and the mapping data reveal a uniform distribution of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, molybdenum, and bismuth across the 0.75CB sample, as depicted in Fig. 2(h). This consistent dispersion provides compelling proof of the successful fabrication of the SCQD/BMO heterojunction, in line with the classification standards set by the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) [36].

HRTEM analysis of BMO and SCQDs/BMO was conducted to investigate the microscopic structure of the composite photocatalytic material. The HRTEM image of BMO is shown in Fig. 2(i), where distinct lattice fringes are observed, indicating that BMO possesses a well-ordered crystalline structure. The lattice spacing of 0.3226 nm corresponds to the γ-BMO phase. The γ-BMO phase typically exhibits a more stable structure and, compared to other polymorphs, often shows superior photocatalytic performance. In particular, its electronic conductivity and photocatalytic activity are stronger in photocatalytic applications. The HRTEM images of the SCQDs/BMO composite material are shown in Fig. 2(j) and (k). The examination indicates that the sample's surface exhibits a grouping pattern of nanorods, displaying lattice distances of 0.21 nm, 0.277 nm, and 0.3158 nm. These values correspond to the (100) crystal plane of SCQDs and the (−2 3 1) and (0 3 2) crystal planes of γ-BMO. This evidence supports the successful synthesis of the composite material and confirms the incorporation of γ-BMO within it [37]. The clear lattice fringes and interface structure in the image indicate that the combination of SCQDs and BMO forms a robust composite structure, significantly enhancing the photocatalytic performance of the composite material. This combination not only optimizes electron transfer and separation but also increases photocatalytic efficiency by providing more active sites. The HRTEM image of carbon quantum dots is shown in Fig. 2(b). By comparing the HRTEM images and EDS analysis of SCQDs, BMO, and SCQDs/BMO, we can observe that the bismuth element mainly exists as Bi3+ in the BMO lattice. The crystal growth occurs through the gradual stacking and growth of BMO nanosheets and nanorods. The introduction of SCQDs does not disrupt the crystal structure of BMO but instead enhances the electron transfer through the interface interaction. The uniform distribution of bismuth indicates that the bismuth element is well-doped and distributed within the BMO material. Additionally, the HRTEM images before and after SCQDs loading show a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the spacing of the graphite (100) plane. The isotherms for N2 adsorption–desorption for samples 0.5CB, 0.75CB, and 1.0CB all conform to the characteristics of type IV isotherms, as depicted in Fig. 2(l). These isotherms exhibit a distinct hysteresis loop at P/P0 = 0.7, suggesting that microporous and mesoporous structures exist. Analysis of the isotherm data using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) technique revealed that 0.75CB possesses the most significant surface area, clocking in at an impressive 27.1535 m2/g, as detailed in Table S1. Additionally, the pore size distribution (PSD) for 0.5CB, 0.75CB, and 1.0CB was investigated with a non-local density functional theory (NLDFT) approach. Their PSDs are predominantly confined to the 0–40 nm range, featuring a mix of mesopores and micropores, which is illustrated in Fig. 2(l).

The XRD patterns for the newly synthesized samples are illustrated in Fig. S2 and 3(a). In Fig. S2, we observe the XRD spectrum of the SCQDs. Notably, a pronounced diffraction peak at 2θ = 27.99 degrees aligns with graphene's (0 0 2) lattice plane (PDF#75–1621). This signifies that the SCQDs boast a considerable degree of crystalline order, a finding that corroborates the data from HRTEM studies. There are firm diffraction peaks at 28.1°, 32.5°, 46.6°, and 55.5°, corresponding to (1 3 1), (2 0 0), (2 0 2), and (1 3 3) crystal planes of γ-BMO (PDF#21–0102), respectively Fig. 3 (a), proving that BMO is successfully loaded onto the SCQDs. The peaks are situated at angles of 27.1°, 37.9°, and 39.8° for 1.0CB, 0.75CB, and 0.5CB samples, aligning with the (0 1 2), (1 0 4), and (110) crystallographic planes of Bismuth (PDF#85–1329), respectively [38]. As a result of the reducibility of SCQD, Bi3+ is converted to Bi, aligning with the findings from the EDS analysis. The Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra of different samples are shown in Fig. 3(b). A strong absorption band at 1600 cm−1 corresponds to C=C stretching, characteristic of sp2 carbon structures. This aligns with the graphite-like structure of SCQDs, [39]. The subtle absorption peak at 2970 cm−1 indicates the stretching vibrations of the –CH2 and –CH units. A slight elevation at 2870 cm−1 points to the presence of –COOH groups. Additionally, the broad peak at 3370 cm−1 corresponds to –OH and –NH stretching vibrations. The pronounced spike at 1110 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching of C=S/S=O and Si-O bonds, highlighting the considerable sulfur and silicon levels present in S. horneri [40] . The peak at 1420 cm−1 is attributed to C=O stretching. Moreover, a pronounced absorption band at 1310 cm−1 indicates the C-N bond's stretching. As for the vibrational mode associated with the 669 cm−1 peak, it's attributed to the stretching of the C-S-C bond [41–43]. The FT-IR examination has unveiled that SCQDs are brimming with oxygen-functional groups and possess properties reminiscent of graphite. Infusing nitrogen, sulfur, and silicon into these SCQDs leads to modifications in their electronic structure, thereby enhancing their capacity for light absorption. The FT-IR spectra of 0.5CB, 0.75CB, 1.0CB, and BMO exhibit similar absorption features. The peaks between 500 and 1000 cm−1 correspond to Bi-O and Mo-O stretching and bending vibrations. [44]. The 1.0CB sample exhibits weaker peak intensity compared to pure BMO, likely due to the significant reduction of Bi3+ to Bi0. The introduction of SCQDs does not generate new peaks in FT-IR spectra, likely due to their low concentration. However, weak absorption peaks at 1735 cm−1 and 1460 cm−1, corresponding to C=O and –COO− vibrations, respectively, are discernible in Fig. S3. These results indicate strong electronic coupling between SCQDs and BMO, confirming robust interfacial interactions. [45]. This electronic interaction facilitates charge transfer, forming an S-scheme heterojunction with enhanced photocatalytic performance. Within this configuration, the SCQDs facilitate electron transfer to BMO, improving charge utilization efficiency in photocatalysis. This reduces charge recombination and enhances photocatalytic efficiency. The XPS analysis depicted in Fig. S4 uncovers the presence of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen on the surface of SCQDs. Fig. S4 (a) shows distinct peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.8 eV, and 287.8 eV in a comprehensive survey. The peak observed at 284.8 eV indicates sp2 hybridized carbon atoms involved in C–C/C=C bonds, suggesting the presence of graphite-like structures within the SCQDs. In contrast, the peak at 285.8 eV is associated with forming C-N/C-O bonds [46], while the peak at 287.8 eV is assigned to C=O. Additionally, a π-π* satellite peak is observed at 293.0 eV. The fine-tuned O 1 s spectrum depicted in Fig. S4(c) exhibits distinct peaks at 531.8 eV and 532.2 eV, which can be linked to the C=O and C-O/O–H bonds, respectively. The spectral analysis depicted in Fig. S4(d) reveals distinct peaks at 400.1 and 399.2 eV, characteristic of the C-N connections in pyridine structures and the N-C=N bonds, respectively. This suggests a successful incorporation of nitrogen into the SCQD framework.

Additionally, a subtle peak emerging at 401.5 eV is presumed to be associated with N–H linkages. The XPS results confirm that the surface of SCQD is brimming with functional groups, playing a pivotal role in the emergence of diverse defect states. These states are crucial in explaining the observed redshift in the fluorescence spectrum. The abundance of surface functionalities, such as C=O, C-O/O–H, and C-N bonds, generates localized electronic states that profoundly affect the fluorescent properties.

The XPS survey spectrum of BMO and the SCQDs/BMO composites, as shown in Fig. S5, predominantly features signals from C, O, N, Bi, and Mo, which attests to the successful incorporation of BMO into SCQDs. Fig. 3(c) presents the C 1 s XPS spectra of SCQDs, 0.50CB, 0.75CB, and 1.0CB samples, where different binding energies correspond to different types of chemical bonds. Deconvolution analysis reveals three characteristic peaks: 284.8 eV corresponds to the C–C bond in the carbon framework, attributed to sp2 or sp3 hybridized carbon, reflecting the fundamental carbon structure of SCQDs and their composites; 286.4 eV corresponds to the C–O bond in ether groups, indicating the presence of oxidized functional groups on the carbon surface, which facilitates electron transfer during photocatalysis; 288.5 eV corresponds to the C=O bond in carbonyl (−C=O) structures, further confirming the enrichment of oxygen-containing functional groups in SCQDs, thereby enhancing their photocatalytic activity. Additionally, compared to SCQDs, the C=O and C–O signals in the composite materials (0.50CB, 0.75CB, and 1.0CB) gradually weaken, which may be attributed to electronic interactions between SCQDs and Bi2MoO6, influencing interfacial charge transfer behavior, promoting the formation of an S-scheme heterojunction, and thereby improving photocatalytic performance. The data presented in Fig. 3(d) reveal that the binding energies at 159.3 eV and 164.4 eV correspond to the Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 transitions, respectively, which serve as precise indicators of the presence of Bi3+ in its oxidized form within the samples that were prepared [47]. Distinctively, the faint peaks of bismuth are discernible at 155.9 eV and 161.2 eV within the SCQDs/BMO composites, indicating that Bi3+ ions have been reduced to elemental bismuth throughout the solvothermal synthesis—a finding in harmony with the XRD data.

Upon examining the Mo3d spectrum depicted in Fig. 3(e), we observe that the energy levels at 235.3 eV and 232.2 eV correspond to the Mo 3d3/2 and Mo 3d5/2 transitions characteristic of the orthorhombic crystal phase within γ-BMO. Concurrently, the two wide-ranging peaks situated at 231.2 eV and 234.0 eV are ascribed to the cubic crystal structure of BMO [48]. The presence of the γ-BMO impurity within the SCQDs/BMO composite may stem from a couple of contributing factors. For one, the conversion of Bi3+ ions to Bi, driven by SCQDs, causes a dip in the ratio of nBi3+ to nMo6+, giving rise to a cubic phase characterized by a diminished Bi/Mo ratio. Additionally, the pH level during synthesis can sway the crystalline structure of BMO, with γ-BMO being more prevalent at higher pH levels. The surface of SCQDs is laden with oxygen-containing functionalities like carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, which tend to lower the pH of the reaction milieu. Furthermore, the reduction of Bi3+ by SCQDs tends to further lower the pH, leading to the formation of an impure orthogonal γ-BMO phase under acidic conditions. In the 0.75CB sample, the noticeable shift in the Mo 3d peaks is primarily attributed to the introduction of SCQDs and the formation of Bi0. SCQDs, as electron donors, transfer electrons to BMO, altering the electron cloud density around Mo, which in turn causes a shift in the Mo 3d binding energy. Additionally, the addition of SCQDs leads to the partial reduction of Bi3+ to Bi0, and the high conductivity of Bi0 introduces localized electric field effects within the BMO matrix, further modifying the electronic environment around Mo and leading to a significant shift in the Mo 3d binding energy. These factors work together, significantly influencing the Mo 3d peak shift, reflecting the role of SCQDs and Bi0 in modulating the electronic structure of BMO. The O 1 s spectrum shown in Fig. 3(f) reveals peaks at 529.9 eV and 531.0 eV for the BMO, 0.5CB, and 0.75CB samples, which correspond to Bi-O and Mo-O bonds, respectively. In contrast, the presence of the O–H peak in the O 1 s spectrum of the 1.0CB sample can be attributed to moisture absorption [49]. The spectral analysis of O1s for the hybrid photocatalysts exhibits a distinct shift toward lower binding energy, signifying a rise in the electron density of the SCQDs/BMO blend. This suggests that incorporating SCQDs can potentially enhance photocatalytic performance.

In the test results, the formation of Bi0 was observed. Through a comprehensive analysis of different samples using XRD, FTIR, and XPS, combined with SEM imaging, we systematically explored the transformation mechanism of Bi3+ to Bi0. Additionally, we conducted an in-depth investigation into the oxidation mechanism of SCQDs by integrating the C 1 s spectra and FTIR analysis of different samples. As a reducing agent, SCQDs facilitate redox reactions with Bi3+ through their abundant surface-active functional groups, such as hydroxyl (–OH), carbonyl (−C=O), and carboxyl (–COOH). During this process, Bi3+ is reduced to elemental Bi0, while SCQDs undergo oxidation.

The transformation mechanism of Bi3+ to Bi0 was first analyzed through XRD patterns. In pure BMO, bismuth exists entirely in the Bi3+ oxidation state. However, with the incorporation of SCQDs, characteristic peaks of Bi0 (PDF#85-1329) gradually emerged, indicating that SCQDs facilitate the partial reduction of Bi3+ to elemental Bi. Moreover, the Bi0 characteristic peaks in 1.0CB were significantly stronger than those in 0.5CB, further confirming the reducing capability of SCQDs. Concurrently, the intensity of BMO characteristic peaks in the 1.0CB sample noticeably weakened, likely due to partial structural disruption or coverage by Bi0.

XPS analysis of the Bi 4f spectrum revealed that in pure BMO, only Bi3+ peaks (Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2) were present, confirming that bismuth exists exclusively in the Bi3+ oxidation state, with no detectable Bi0. In 0.5CB and 0.75CB, Bi mainly remained as Bi3+, but weak signals of Bi0 (low binding energy peaks of Bi 4f) were observed, suggesting that a small amount of Bi3+ had been partially reduced. In the 1.0CB sample, the Bi0 characteristic peaks were further enhanced, while the Bi3+ peaks significantly weakened, indicating that an excessive amount of SCQDs induced extensive reduction of Bi3+. Additionally, excessive Bi0 may have aggregated on the sample surface, potentially blocking active sites and negatively impacting photocatalytic performance.

SEM analysis further supported these findings. The BMO surface exhibited a typical flower-like microstructure without noticeable particle attachment or significant morphological changes. In 0.5CB, a small amount of SCQDs adhered to the BMO surface without significantly altering its flower-like structure, leading to a limited degree of Bi3+ reduction. In 0.75CB, SCQDs were uniformly distributed on the BMO surface, partially covering the flower-like structure and forming a well-dispersed Bi3+/Bi0 synergistic structure. However, in 1.0CB, excessive SCQDs led to aggregation, with a large amount of Bi0 covering the BMO surface, ultimately disrupting the original flower-like morphology and resulting in the over-reduction of Bi3+ to Bi0.

The mechanism by which SCQDs reduce Bi3+ can be explained by the role of surface-active functional groups such as hydroxyl (–OH) and carbonyl (−C=O) acting as electron donors. These groups facilitate the transfer of electrons to Bi3+, reducing it to elemental Bi, while the SCQDs themselves undergo oxidation in the process. Infrared spectroscopic analysis reveals that SCQDs are rich in functional groups such as –OH, C=O, and –COOH, which participate in redox reactions by donating electrons for the reduction of Bi3+. After oxidation, the SCQDs exhibit a decrease in surface-active groups, as evidenced by the diminished intensity of –OH, C=O, and –COOH peaks in the composite materials, confirming the involvement of hydroxyl groups in the electron transfer process.

This observation supports the conclusion that SCQDs undergo partial oxidation during the composite formation. In the redox process, SCQDs act as electron donors, transferring electrons through their functional groups (e.g., C=O) to reduce Bi3+ to Bi0, following the reaction:Bi3+ + SCQD-OH → Bi0 + SCQD-C=O.

Furthermore, XPS analysis of the C 1s spectrum provides additional evidence of SCQD oxidation. Before the reaction, the C 1s spectrum shows characteristic peaks at 284.8 eV (C–C/C=C), corresponding to sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon bonds in the SCQD framework; 286.4 eV (C–O), attributed to ether bonds; and 288.5 eV (C=O), indicative of carbonyl groups. After composite formation, the intensity of the C=O peak at 288.5 eV significantly decreases, suggesting partial consumption of carbonyl groups during the reaction. Additionally, the C–C peak weakens while the C–O peak strengthens, indicating that oxidation of the SCQD surface results in the partial conversion of C–C bonds to C–O bonds. This trend aligns with the function of SCQDs as electron donors in the reduction of Bi3+.

To assess the efficacy of the photocatalytic activity of 0.5CB, 0.75CB, 1.0CB, and BMO when exposed to visible light, the compound CIP was selected as a model contaminant. The degradation rates are illustrated in Fig. 4 (a). Minimal adsorption of CIP occurred in the absence of light due to the low surface area of the freshly prepared samples, suggesting that photocatalytic breakdown is the predominant mechanism. As the duration of exposure to light increased, these samples systematically broke down the CIP. Notably, 0.75CB demonstrated a superior capability for CIP degradation compared to the other samples, achieving a 97.7 % reduction after 180 min of illumination. In comparison, the degradation rates for 0.5CB, 1.0CB, and BMO were 71.5 %, 54.4 %, and 50.3 %, respectively. This significant enhancement in photocatalytic efficiency highlights the optimized charge separation and transfer mechanisms in 0.75CB, facilitated by the synergistic effects of SCQDs and elemental Bi. The degradation efficiency of these photocatalysts was ranked as follows: 0.75CB > 0.5CB > 1.0CB > BMO, which reinforces the notion that incorporating SCQD onto the surface of BMO significantly enhances its photocatalytic capabilities. However, the 1.0CB sample demonstrated a slower rate of degradation when compared to the 0.75CB sample, largely due to an excess of Bi molecules taking up the adsorption sites. This saturation effect reduced both the adsorption capacity and the count of accessible photocatalytic sites [50]. Moreover, an abundance of bismuth has a tendency to clump together, forming hotspots for the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers. This clustering accelerates the rate at which electrons and holes recombine, which consequently diminishes their contribution to the photocatalytic process [50]. The efficiency of photocatalysis is also affected by various environmental factors, including the amount of photocatalyst used, the concentration of CIP, and the pH level. As depicted in Fig. 4(b), an increase in CIP concentration from 10 mg/L to 30 mg/L results in a decline in the degradation rate, dropping from 97.7 % to 67.7 %. This reduction can be explained by the accumulation of intermediate by-products and the limited availability of active sites for photocatalytic reactions, both of which collectively hinder the efficiency of the process [51]. The effect of pH on photocatalytic efficiency was evaluated by testing SCQDs/BMO at pH levels 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11. The findings reveal that the degradation rate of CIP reached its peak at the pH level depicted in Fig. 4(c). Notably, both highly acidic and alkaline environments were found to diminish the effectiveness of degradation. This reduction in efficiency can be attributed to the formation of positively or negatively charged zwitterions in CIP due to its piperazine and quinolone structures, which alter the intermediate products and degradation pathways under varying pH conditions [52]. Fig. 4(d) shows that as the quantity of photocatalyst is increased, the degradation rate of CIP also rises. At a concentration of 50 mg, the degradation efficiency of CIP reaches a remarkable 97.7 %, thanks to the increased availability of active sites. Additionally, the photocatalytic degradation of CIP follows a pseudo-first-order kinetic model [53], as shown in Fig. 4(e). The degradation rate constants for 0.5CB, 0.75CB, 1.0CB, and BMO are 0.0064 min−1, 0.0092 min−1, 0.0041 min−1, and 0.0035 min−1, respectively (Table S2), among which, 0.75CB exhibits the fastest degradation rate, with a rate constant of 2.6 times that of BMO. To assess the recycling potential of 0.75CB, we carried out three rounds of photocatalytic degradation trials under identical settings. The findings revealed that, following these cycles, the degradation efficiency of CIP using 0.75CB stayed over 75 % within a span of 180 min, as depicted in Fig. 4(f). This suggests a notable level of stability and impressive reusability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible degradation pathways of CIP and toxicity assessment
ce:section.ce:para: To shed light on the breakdown process of CIP through the action of SCQDs/BMO, an HPLC-MS investigation was carried out on potential intermediate molecules at varied reaction intervals (0, 90, and 180 min). The combined mass spectrometry data is illustrated in Fig. S6, revealing that the peak attributed to CIP (m/z = 332) decreases gradually with increasing exposure time. On the flip side, the peaks for the lesser compounds experience an initial rise before they, too, begin to decline with ongoing light exposure, indicating a step-by-step degradation of CIP into these minor components, culminating in their conversion to inorganic substances. Through HPLC-MS, we pinpointed nine principal intermediates in the photocatalytic decomposition of CIP. The probable degradation routes are outlined in Fig. 5 . In the first pathway, reactive oxygen species (ROS) target the piperazine moiety, oxidizing CIP (m/z 332) to create P1 (m/z 362). Following this, ROS continues to assault the N-C bond in the piperazine ring, yielding P2 (m/z 334), which then disintegrates into P3 (m/z 291) and subsequently breaks down into P4 (m/z 245). In the second pathway, ROS initiates the fragmentation of CIP's pyridine ring, F bond, and carboxyl group, generating P5 (m/z 228), which then undergoes ring opening to form P6 (m/z 114). Pathway III involves ROS-induced substitution reactions on the aromatic structure of CIP, swapping the F atom on the aromatic ring with an –OH group to produce P7 (m/z 330). The decomposition of P7′s piperazine ring results in P8 (m/z 318), which undergoes decarboxylation to become P9 (m/z 274). Ultimately, the piperazine group is entirely cleaved, and the resulting intermediates degrade further into minuscule molecules, culminating in the mineralization of H2O and CO2 [54–56] .

The risks associated with photodegradation by-products were assessed using the Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T) [57]. Utilizing QSAR models, which scrutinize the correlation between molecular structure and biological reactivity, researchers established the concentrations lethal to 50 % of the test subjects for the fathead minnow (96-hour LC50), daphnia (48-hour LC50), tetrahymena pyriformis (48-hour IGC50), as well as the oral LD50 for rats. The findings in Table S3 indicate a considerable rise in the LC50, LD50, and IGC50 levels for the chemical intermediates, as opposed to the compound of interest, CIP. This points to a notable decrease in the acute toxicity of CIP following photocatalytic processing. Additionally, the investigation explored the bioaccumulation factor (BAF) and potential mutagenicity. The data depicted in Fig. 6 reveal a pronounced decrease in the BAF for confident intermediates throughout the photodegradation of CIP. In comparison to the mutagenic CIP, it was observed that the bulk of these intermediates exhibited no mutagenic properties. Consequently, these findings underscore a substantial reduction in the ecological dangers associated with CIP after its photodegradation using the SCQDS/BMO heterojunction photocatalyst.

A multitude of investigations and experiments were conducted to delve into the intricacies of the photocatalytic mechanism and the performance of SCQDs/BMO composites. In the ensuing sections, we'll dive into the composite's optical characteristics, the dynamics of charge transfer, and the degradation pathways involved in the photocatalytic process, and we'll propose a mechanism grounded in the findings collected. We utilized UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) to determine the light absorption features of these photocatalysts. As illustrated in Fig. 7 (a), it's clear that the absorption edge of the photocatalysts shifts toward longer wavelengths as the concentration of SCQDs increases, which points to a redshift. This shift suggests an interaction between SCQDs and BMO, resulting in an enhanced visible light response in the SCQDs/BMO composites. Additionally, the presence of Bi further improves light absorption, potentially boosting their photocatalytic efficiency. Concurrently, the expanded absorption spectrum of the photocatalysts fosters a more effective separation and mobility of the charge carriers generated by photoexcitation, which in turn has the potential to augment photocatalytic performance. The band gap energies (Eg) can be determined via the Tauc plot: (αhν)1/n = A(hν − Eg), where α is the absorption coefficient, hν is the energy, A is a constant, and n = 1/2 [58]. The graphical representation in Fig. 7(b) shows that the energy band gaps for various concentrations, including 0.5CB, 0.75CB, 1.0CB, and the pure BMO, are 2.78 eV, 2.49 eV, 1.99 eV, and 2.76 eV, respectively. When used independently, the hybrid SCQDs/BMO construct demonstrates a notably narrower band gap than BMO. This decrease in band gap leads to a better absorption of visible light and facilitates the separation of photo-induced charge carriers, significantly boosting the material's photocatalytic efficiency [8]. A thorough examination of the ultraviolet absorption spectrum and band gap energy was conducted to delve deeper into the light-interaction characteristics of SCQDs. The graphical representation in Fig. 7(c) illustrates that the UV absorption range of SCQDs spans approximately 200 to 750 nm. Moreover, the band gap energy of these quantum dots was calculated to be 2.83 electron volts, as indicated by the Tauc plot featured in the inset of Fig. 7(c). The VB-XPS and VB-UPS techniques established the valence band potential at 2.75 eV for SCQDs and 3.03 eV for BMO. As depicted in Fig. 7(d) and 5(e), respectively, the Valence Band Edge (EVB) relative to the standard hydrogen electrode (EVB vs. NHE) was determined using the provided formula (EVB vs. NHE = Φ + EVB-XPS/VB-UPS − 4.44)[59], The electron work function of the apparatus, denoted as Φ, is 4.50 eV. Consequently, the valence band (EVB) energies for the SCQDs and BMO were ascertained at 2.81 eV and 3.09 eV, respectively, when referenced to the standard hydrogen electrode (NHE). The conduction band (ECB) potentials for SCQDs and BMO were estimated at 0.02 eV and 0.33 eV, derived from the equation ECB = EVB − Eg. The energy band diagrams for BMO and SCQDs are depicted in Fig. 7(f), showcasing the likelihood of an S-scheme heterojunction formation between these two materials.

The fundamental steps in photocatalytic reactions generally involve light absorption, followed by the separation and transport of charge carriers, namely electrons and holes. Multiple methodologies were utilized to assess the solar conversion efficiency of the 0.75CB and BMO compounds, such as conducting transient photocurrent tests, tracking the photoluminescence (PL) emission over time, and performing electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) examinations. The chart in Fig. 8 (a) reveals that the photocurrent density of the 0.75CB sample surpassed that of BMO. This suggests that blending BMO with SCQDs facilitates the separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs, subsequently enhancing the photocatalytic performance [60]. Additionally, the PL profiles of both bulk BMO and the SCQDs/BMO hybrid were scrutinized, as depicted in Fig. 8(b). It's usually observed that an uptick in PL brightness is linked to a quicker rate of electron-hole pair recombination. Significantly, the PL luminosity of the SCQDs/BMO amalgam is substantially dimmer compared to pure BMO, hinting that incorporating SCQDs leads to a drop in the recombination efficiency of the photogenerated charge carriers. In particular, a concentration of 0.75CB exhibited the weakest fluorescence, pointing to a slower electron-hole recombination rate and a more effective charge separation. Such attributes are favorable for a boost in photocatalytic activity. The EIS readings shed light on the resistance characteristics of the photocatalyst, with lower impedance values signifying improved conductivity [29]. In Fig. 8(c), the impedance radius for 0.75CB is less than that of BMO, suggesting that 0.75CB exhibits a greater efficiency in separating photogenerated carriers and a quicker electron transport rate in comparison to BMO, which further supports the findings from the photoluminescence results [61]. The luminescent behavior of SCQDs, as depicted in Fig. S8, exhibits their proficiency in emitting light across a lengthy spectral range—spanning from 450 nm to 600 nm—upon stimulation with shorter-wavelength radiation (in the 300–400 nm bracket). Significantly, the emitted light's spectrum undergoes a redshift with the escalation of the excitation wavelength. This phenomenon can be ascribed to the quantum confinement effect and the surface imperfections within the SCQDs/BMO matrix. Such observations affirm that SCQDs can transform high-energy UV or blue light into lower-energy visible light, thereby optimizing the light energy conversion efficiency of the SCQDs/BMO composite and boosting its photocatalytic performance [10].

To delve deeper into the electron transfer dynamics at the heterojunction interface, we scrutinized the sample's work function (Φ) using ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS). Fig. 8 in panels (d-e) of Fig. 8 reveals that the respective cutoff energy levels for BMO and SCQDs clock in at 17.1 and 18.35 eV. By subtracting the helium I excitation energy of 21.22 eV, we calculated the work function values to be 4.12 eV for BMO and 2.87 eV for SCQDs. Notably, BMO boasts a substantial Φ paired with a low electron Fermi level (EF), whereas SCQDs exhibit a lower Φ coupled with a higher EF. The data presented in Fig. 8(g) illustrate that when BMO and SCQDs are in close contact, electrons (e-) from the SCQDs migrate toward the BMO, proceeding until an equilibrium EF is reached. This migration and the robust chemical interaction at the interface can precipitate the development of an IEF spanning the negatively charged BMO and the positively charged SCQDs [62,63].

Furthermore, the band edges of SCQDs are elevated due to a decrease in free electrons, whereas those of BMO decline as free electrons accumulate. Under visible light irradiation, both BMO and SCQDs are excited. The interplay of the IEF and the bending of energy bands, combined with the role of elemental Bi in electron transfer, collectively facilitates the transfer of photogenerated electrons from the conduction band (CB) of BMO to Bi and subsequently to the holes (h+) in the valence band (VB) of SCQDs, thus forming an effective S-scheme heterojunction. Studies were carried out to identify and understand the behavior of reactive species involved in the photocatalytic process. Fig. 8(f) illustrates using four quenchers: 1,4-benzoquinone, triethanolamine, isopropanol, and AgNO3. The data reveals that including AgNO3 and 1,4-benzoquinone substantially slowed the decomposition of CIP, reducing it to 0.75 times the rate observed with CB. This outcome underscores the vital contributions of electrons (e−) and superoxide radicals (•O2 −) to the photocatalytic breakdown of CIP. Building on the analysis presented, we propose an S-scheme photocatalytic mechanism for SCQDs/BMO, illustrated in Fig. 8(g).

In the absence of light, the energy field difference between BMO and SCQDs instigates the movement of electrons during their interaction, resulting in an IEF directed from SCQDs to BMO, which balances the energy field at the interface. Concurrently, this leads to a bending of the energy bands: SCQDs exhibit an upward bend while BMO bends downward due to electron depletion and accumulation, respectively. During the photocatalytic reaction, the IEF acts as a powerful driving force, promoting directed charge transfer and effectively separating photo-excited carriers in space. Importantly, SCQDs function as electron acceptors to facilitate the S-scheme charge transfer and as highly effective photosensitizers, boosting light responsiveness. The high conductivity of Bi further enhances the electron transport capability of the system, working synergistically with the internal electric field to promote the separation and migration of charge carriers. This optimization improves the interfacial charge transfer pathway between BMO and SCQDs [64]. Remarkably, the conduction band of SCQDs harbors high-energy electrons, while the valence band of BMO is the repository for holes. It is within these bands that reduction and oxidation processes yield electrons (e−) and superoxide radicals (•O2 −), respectively, which efficiently dismantle CIP into harmless compounds Fig. 9 . CIP's photocatalytic decomposition process with SCQDs/BMO heterostructure can be succinctly described as follows:

(7) h+ VB(BMO) + H2O / OH − → • OH
(8) CIP + •O2 −/•OH → Small molecules + CO2 + H2O

In this study, we developed a novel SCQDs/BMO photocatalyst crafted by integrating marine-algae-derived SCQDs from S. horneri with Bi2MoO6 to create an S-scheme heterojunction. This innovative setup is tailored to bolster the photocatalytic degradation of CIP with remarkable efficiency. The synergy between SCQDs and BMO creates an interfacial electric field, enhancing the photocatalyst's light absorption capabilities, promoting efficient charge separation, and facilitating carrier mobility. This results in the SCQDs/BMO demonstrating outstanding photocatalytic prowess, with a peak degradation efficiency of 97.7 % for CIP within 180 min and impressive recyclability and stability. During the photocatalytic breakdown of CIP, ·OH and •O2 − are predominantly the active species, breaking down the compound into minute molecules through three distinct pathways. Consequently, the SCQDs/BMO stands out as an eco-conscious, affordable, and exceptionally effective photocatalyst, holding great promise to purify antibiotic-laden wastewater.
